summary: Gruber documents and quantifies the long-running suppression of Daring Fireball articles on Hacker News. Using a new tool by Michael Lynch that ranks personal blogs by HN popularity, he shows DF ranked #2-3 historically but has plummeted to #72 in the last four years. He traces the pattern from the mid-2010s, when DF articles began mysteriously disappearing from the HN front page after gaining traction, to the present day where they almost never surface at all. He notes occasional exceptions that make the suppression more puzzling, and concludes that the unexplained nature of the blacklisting is what truly bothers him, calling it cowardly rather than censorial.
key_points: Daring Fireball ranked #2-3 among personal blogs on Hacker News from 2007 through 2021, but has dropped to #72 in the 2021-2025 period
DF articles consistently get suppressed after gaining any traction on HN's front page, with most recent submissions garnering 0-3 comments
Occasional exceptions slip through, like the DRM screenshot post getting 208 comments, making the pattern seem manual rather than programmatic
Gruber has been noticing and documenting this suppression since at least the mid-2010s, with public mentions dating to December 2017
His core grievance is not the suppression itself but that it has never been explained or acknowledged by Hacker News
money_quotes: What bothers me isn't so much that Daring Fireball is shitlisted at Hacker News — even though I really did enjoy reading the commentary on my posts back when they regularly surfaced there. What bothers me is that it's unexplained. Which, ultimately, seems not so much censorial as just cowardly.

Maybe I've lost my fastball, and I just don't write so good no more. Or maybe it's not me, but the Hacker News audience that has changed in recent years. But it seems to me there's something fishy going on.
Once any traction occurs, something happens and poof, they're gone from the Hacker News front page.
There's clearly no programmatic blacklisting that keeps Daring Fireball articles from being submitted, or from gaining a few comments. But once any traction occurs, something happens.
You'd think there'd be a certain kinship between decades-old websites, typeset in small-point Verdana, which stubbornly refuse to update their general layout and design.
It sounds a bit conspiratorial, but for several years now it's seemed clear to me that Hacker News has Daring Fireball in some sort of graylist.
key_insight: Daring Fireball's fall from the #2 most popular personal blog on Hacker News to #72 over four years, despite no obvious decline in quality, points to systematic unexplained suppression that HN has never acknowledged.
